summary:
Acid etching removes the smear layer from dentin surfaces; however, increasing surface tension has not been identified or implied by any studies related to its use for dental applications such as bonding agents adhering better after treatment due increased exposure rather than enhanced adhesion through higher surface energy which isn't directly achieved via simple chemical dissolution processes involved here during preparation steps prior restoration placement procedures like composite fillings etcetera .
The statement about 'increasing' surface tension stands out because no study indicates an intentional mechanism designed specifically towards raising interfacial tensions between materials at microscopic levels without additional treatments beyond basic mechanical cleaning actions performed alongside acidic exposures typically used clinically . The other options align well within known physiological responses expected when using acids topically before restorative work begins so they can be ruled out confidently based upon current understanding derived primarily form scientific research into these techniques over decades now .

To summarize Agent1’s reasoning more concisely while focusing only on critical aspects pertinent to answering "all being true EXCEPT":

Acid etching exposes collagen fibers and opens dentinal tubules but does NOT inherently aim to increase tooth enamel/surface tension significantly enough to affect bond strength independently—this last point distinguishes itself among accurate statements concerning typical uses and mechanisms associated with acid etch protocols in modern dentistry practice. To identify what doesn’t hold up under scrutiny relative to standard procedure knowledge requires recognizing how specific claims relate back against broader accepted facts surrounding material
